project_summary: Web application (localhost-first) that renders a hierarchical graph of Aitri artifacts (Epics, Features, User Stories, Test Cases) with status indicators, zoom/pan navigation, expand/collapse, and cross-hierarchy dependency edges. Supports loading projects from a GitHub repository URL or from a local filesystem path via a minimal Node.js server. Multiple projects can be registered and switched via a sidebar.

product_analysis.north_star_kpi: User identifies the status of any artifact and its dependencies within 30 seconds of opening the app, without opening any file
product_analysis.jtbd: When reviewing a product's spec pipeline, the PM/developer hires this tool to instantly see the full artifact hierarchy, what's done, what's blocked, and what depends on what — without opening multiple JSON files
product_analysis.guardrail_metric: Initial graph render with 50 nodes must complete in ≤3 seconds on a standard desktop browser (Chrome/Firefox latest)

no_go_zone: No authentication or user accounts — no login, sessions, roles, or access control
No editing of artifacts from the UI — the graph is read-only
No database or remote persistence — project list may use localStorage or in-memory only
No mobile-first or responsive design — targets desktop browsers at ≥1280px viewport width
No real-time updates or WebSocket connections — data is loaded on demand, not pushed
No private GitHub repositories — no OAuth flow, no GitHub token management for POC
No cloud deployment for POC — runs on localhost only

user_personas.goal: Review which Features and User Stories are approved, pending, or drifted before a sprint review or demo, without asking the dev team to open files
user_personas.pain_point: Currently must open multiple JSON/MD files and mentally reconstruct the hierarchy; misses drift or gaps under deadline pressure

user_personas.goal: Verify Test Case coverage per Feature and spot cross-artifact dependencies before starting implementation
user_personas.pain_point: Dependency chains between User Stories are invisible in file-based workflows; discovering a missing dependency late causes rework

functional_requirements.title: Hierarchical graph render from loaded project data
functional_requirements.description: Given a loaded Aitri project (from GitHub or local), the app renders a directed hierarchical graph with nodes for Epics, Features, User Stories, and Test Cases connected parent-to-child (Epic → Feature → User Story → Test Case). All nodes and edges defined in the source data must appear on load.
acceptance_criteria: Given a project with 2 Epics, 4 Features, 8 User Stories, and 8 Test Cases, when the project is loaded and selected, then all 22 nodes are visible in the graph within 3 seconds
Given the rendered graph, when inspected, then each Epic has visible directed edges to its Feature children, each Feature to its User Stories, and each User Story to its Test Cases
Given a project source that returns malformed or empty JSON, when the project is loaded, then a visible error message appears in the graph canvas area (not a blank page)

functional_requirements.title: Artifact status visual indicator per node
functional_requirements.description: Each node displays a visual indicator (color fill or border plus icon) for its artifact status. Supported statuses: pending, in_progress, approved, complete, drift. A persistent legend maps each status to its visual representation.
acceptance_criteria: Given nodes with statuses pending, in_progress, approved, complete, and drift, when rendered, then each status uses a unique color or icon distinct from all others
Given any two status colors in the graph, when measured, then contrast ratio between the status color and the node background is ≥3:1
Given the rendered graph, when inspected, then a visible legend mapping each status to its color/icon is present on screen at all times

functional_requirements.title: Zoom in/out on graph canvas
functional_requirements.description: The user can zoom in and out using mouse scroll wheel and on-screen +/- controls. Zoom scales graph content without distorting labels or edges.
acceptance_criteria: Given the rendered graph, when the user scrolls the mouse wheel up, then the graph scales up and node labels remain at ≥10px font size
Given the rendered graph, when the user scrolls down, then the graph scales down until all nodes fit in view
Given on-screen +/- buttons, when clicked, then zoom level changes by ≥10% per click
Given any zoom level, when displayed, then edge lines are ≥1px thick

functional_requirements.title: Pan (drag) navigation on graph canvas
functional_requirements.description: The user can drag the canvas to pan. Panning on empty canvas areas must not interfere with node click interactions.
acceptance_criteria: Given the rendered graph, when the user clicks and drags an empty canvas area, then the viewport translates in the drag direction
Given a node is clicked without dragging, when the pointer is released in the same position (±3px), then no pan occurs
Given the user has panned, when the mouse is released, then the graph stays at the new position without snapping back

Given the rendered graph, when the user holds down the mouse button on the canvas, then the cursor changes to a grabbing hand (cursor: grabbing) for the duration of the drag, and reverts to the default cursor on mouse release

functional_requirements.title: Expand/collapse sub-tree on node click
functional_requirements.description: Parent nodes (Epics, Features, User Stories) can be collapsed to hide their descendant sub-tree. The collapsed state is reflected visually on the node.
acceptance_criteria: Given a Feature node with 3 child User Story nodes visible, when the user clicks it, then all 3 User Story nodes and their Test Case children are hidden
Given a collapsed node, when clicked again, then all previously hidden descendants reappear in their original positions
Given a collapsed node, when rendered, then a visual indicator (e.g. '+' icon or dimmed border) distinguishes it from an expanded node
Given a leaf node (no children), when clicked, then no collapse/expand behavior occurs

functional_requirements.title: Dependency edges between non-hierarchical nodes
functional_requirements.description: Any node can declare dependencies on other nodes (cross-hierarchy). These are rendered as visually distinct edges from parent-child hierarchy edges.
acceptance_criteria: Given two nodes with a declared dependency, when the graph renders, then a directed edge connects them and is visually distinct from hierarchy edges (different color or dashed/dotted line)
Given a dependency edge and a hierarchy edge visible simultaneously, when inspected, then a user can distinguish them without a tooltip
Given a dependency edge, when rendered, then a directional arrowhead indicates the direction of the dependency

functional_requirements.title: Node detail tooltip on hover
functional_requirements.description: Hovering over a node shows a tooltip with the node's key metadata: id, title, type, status, and dependencies (if any).
acceptance_criteria: Given any node in the graph, when hovered, then a tooltip appears within 300ms showing: id, title, type, and status
Given a node with dependencies, when the tooltip is shown, then dependency IDs are listed
Given an active tooltip, when the pointer moves away from the node, then the tooltip disappears within 500ms

functional_requirements.title: Load project from GitHub repository URL
functional_requirements.description: The user can register a project by entering a public GitHub repository URL. The app constructs raw content URLs using the known Aitri artifact paths (spec/01_REQUIREMENTS.json, spec/03_TEST_CASES.json, .aitri) and fetches them client-side. No server required for GitHub projects.
acceptance_criteria: Given a valid public GitHub repo URL (e.g. https://github.com/owner/repo), when the user submits it, then the app fetches spec/01_REQUIREMENTS.json from raw.githubusercontent.com and renders the graph within 5 seconds
Given a GitHub URL pointing to a repo without Aitri artifacts, when fetched, then a visible error message states 'No Aitri artifacts found' in the graph area
Given a GitHub URL for a non-existent repo, when fetched, then a visible error message states the repo was not found (HTTP 404)

functional_requirements.title: Load project from local filesystem path
functional_requirements.description: The user can register a project by entering an absolute local filesystem path. The minimal Node.js server exposes GET /api/project?path=... which reads the Aitri artifacts at that path and returns them as JSON. The frontend calls this endpoint to load the project.
acceptance_criteria: Given a valid local path to an Aitri project directory, when the user submits it, then the server reads spec/01_REQUIREMENTS.json from that path and the graph renders within 3 seconds
Given a path that does not contain Aitri artifacts, when submitted, then the server returns a descriptive error and the app displays 'No Aitri artifacts found at this path'
Given a path that does not exist on the filesystem, when submitted, then the server returns 404 and the app displays a visible error message

functional_requirements.title: Project sidebar — list and switch projects
functional_requirements.description: A persistent sidebar lists all registered projects (name + source type: GitHub or local). The user selects a project from the sidebar to load its graph in the main canvas. Only one project is shown at a time. Projects can be removed from the sidebar.
acceptance_criteria: Given 3 registered projects, when the sidebar is rendered, then all 3 appear as selectable items showing name and source type (GitHub or local)
Given a project item in the sidebar, when the user clicks it, then the graph canvas updates to show that project's artifact graph within 3 seconds
Given the currently active project, when displayed in the sidebar, then it is visually highlighted (e.g. bold, background color, or active indicator) to distinguish it from inactive projects
Given a project in the sidebar, when the user clicks a remove/delete control, then the project is removed from the list and, if it was active, the canvas is cleared

functional_requirements.title: Register new project via input form
functional_requirements.description: A UI element (button or inline form) allows the user to add a new project by entering either a GitHub URL or a local path. The form validates the input format before submitting.
acceptance_criteria: Given the add-project form, when the user enters a string that does not start with 'https://github.com/' or '/' (absolute path), then a validation error message (≥10 characters) appears inline within 100ms and no network request is made
Given a valid GitHub URL or local path, when submitted, then the project is added to the sidebar within 500ms and auto-selected
Given a successful project load, when the form is dismissed, then the input field value is empty (length = 0)

functional_requirements.title: Fit-to-screen / reset view control
functional_requirements.description: An on-screen button resets zoom and pan so all nodes are visible within the viewport.
acceptance_criteria: Given the user has zoomed in or panned away, when they click the Fit/Reset button, then all nodes become visible within the viewport within 500ms
Given the fit action completes, when the graph is inspected, then no node is clipped or outside the visible canvas area

user_stories.i_want: to select a project and see all its artifacts rendered as a graph
user_stories.so_that: I can understand the full structure of the product without opening any files

acceptance_criteria.given: a project with 2 Epics, 4 Features, 8 User Stories, and 8 Test Cases is selected in the sidebar
acceptance_criteria.then: all 22 nodes are visible in the graph canvas within 3 seconds

acceptance_criteria.when: the user inspects an Epic node
acceptance_criteria.then: visible directed edges connect it to each of its Feature children

user_stories.i_want: to see the status of each artifact visually on its node
user_stories.so_that: I can identify approved, pending, and drifted items at a glance without reading labels

acceptance_criteria.given: a graph with nodes of all 5 statuses
acceptance_criteria.then: each status has a unique color or icon and a persistent on-screen legend maps each status to its visual representation

user_stories.i_want: to zoom in on a dense graph area
user_stories.so_that: I can read labels and inspect connections in crowded sections

acceptance_criteria.when: the user scrolls the mouse wheel upward 5 times
acceptance_criteria.then: the graph zooms in and node labels remain at ≥10px

user_stories.i_want: to drag the canvas after zooming in
user_stories.so_that: I can navigate to off-screen nodes freely

acceptance_criteria.given: the user has zoomed in so that some nodes are off-screen
acceptance_criteria.when: the user clicks and drags an empty canvas area
acceptance_criteria.then: the viewport pans and previously off-screen nodes come into view

user_stories.i_want: to collapse an Epic's sub-tree when I only care about top-level status

acceptance_criteria.given: a Feature node with 3 child User Stories visible
acceptance_criteria.when: the user clicks the Feature node
acceptance_criteria.then: the 3 User Stories and their Test Case children are hidden and the Feature shows a collapsed indicator

user_stories.i_want: to see dependency edges between User Stories that block each other
user_stories.so_that: I can identify implementation order before sprint planning

acceptance_criteria.given: two User Stories with a declared dependency
acceptance_criteria.then: a directed edge connects them with a style (dashed or different color) distinct from hierarchy edges, with a visible arrowhead

user_stories.i_want: to hover over a node and see its key metadata
user_stories.so_that: I can confirm id, type, and status without switching to a file

acceptance_criteria.then: a tooltip appears within 300ms showing id, title, type, and status

user_stories.i_want: to enter a GitHub repo URL and see its Aitri graph
user_stories.so_that: I can inspect any public Aitri project without cloning it locally

acceptance_criteria.given: a valid public GitHub repo URL with Aitri artifacts
acceptance_criteria.then: the app fetches the artifacts and renders the graph within 5 seconds

user_stories.i_want: to enter a local folder path and see its Aitri graph
user_stories.so_that: I can inspect a local Aitri project I'm actively working on

acceptance_criteria.given: a valid local path to an Aitri project
acceptance_criteria.then: the Node.js server reads the artifacts and the graph renders within 3 seconds

user_stories.i_want: to see all my registered projects in a sidebar and switch between them
user_stories.so_that: I can compare project status across multiple products without re-entering their paths

acceptance_criteria.then: all 3 appear with name and source type (GitHub or local)
the graph updates to show that project's artifacts within 3 seconds

user_stories.i_want: to add a new project by typing its GitHub URL or local path
user_stories.so_that: I can register any Aitri project quickly

acceptance_criteria.when: the user enters a string that is neither a GitHub URL nor an absolute path
acceptance_criteria.then: a validation error appears inline and no fetch is attempted

user_stories.i_want: to reset the view to see the full graph with one click
user_stories.so_that: I can recover quickly after getting lost in a zoomed-in view

acceptance_criteria.given: the user has zoomed in and panned away
acceptance_criteria.then: all nodes are visible within the viewport within 500ms and no node is clipped

non_functional_requirements.requirement: Initial graph render with ≥22 nodes must complete within 3 seconds of project selection, measured from the moment the artifact data is available in the browser
non_functional_requirements.acceptance_criteria: Graph is fully painted and interactive within 3 seconds as measured by browser DevTools Performance tab with the mock dataset

non_functional_requirements.requirement: The app must start with a single command ('node server.js' or equivalent) with no build step. The Node.js server must be a single file with no npm dependencies beyond Node.js built-ins
non_functional_requirements.acceptance_criteria: App is fully functional after running 'node server.js' from the project root with no prior 'npm install' or build command

non_functional_requirements.requirement: The graph layout must be automatically computed and must not produce overlapping node labels at default zoom for the mock dataset on a 1280px-wide viewport
non_functional_requirements.acceptance_criteria: At default zoom on a 1280px viewport, no two node labels overlap as verified by visual inspection

non_functional_requirements.requirement: All error states (missing artifacts, network failure, invalid path) must display a descriptive message in the UI — no blank canvas, no uncaught exceptions in the console
non_functional_requirements.acceptance_criteria: For each of the 3 error scenarios (missing artifacts, 404, malformed JSON), the app shows a visible error message ≥10 characters in the graph area and the browser console shows no uncaught exceptions

non_functional_requirements.requirement: The local Node.js server must reject path traversal attempts: any path containing '..' must return HTTP 400 with no file content
non_functional_requirements.acceptance_criteria: GET /api/project?path=../../etc/passwd returns HTTP 400 and no file content is returned

constraints: Node.js built-ins only for the server — no npm packages, no external dependencies
No paid or proprietary libraries — open source only (MIT/Apache/BSD) for frontend
No frontend build step (CDN imports acceptable for graph library)
GitHub projects: public repos only (no auth)
technology_preferences: Graph rendering: Cytoscape.js or D3.js — Architect decides based on ease of hierarchical layout + dependency edges + expand/collapse
Frontend: Vanilla JS preferred; React via CDN acceptable if complexity warrants it
Server: Node.js built-in http module only (no Express, no Fastify)
Single HTML entry point (index.html) loading JS/CSS as sibling files
